summary:
The earliest evidence for cheese production has been found at Neolithic sites in Poland and the wider European and Near Eastern world, with lipid residue analysis of ceramic vessels identifying milk fats that may be associated with cheese-making activities dating to approximately 6500 to 6000 BCE. Cheese — fermented, coagulated, and concentrated milk — represents an important solution to the problem of lactose intolerance, which was nearly universal among adult humans prior to the spread of lactase persistence mutations in dairy-farming populations. By converting raw milk into cheese, early farmers could access the nutritional value of dairy without the digestive consequences of consuming large quantities of fresh milk.

The ability to make cheese depends on the availability of rennet — enzymes that coagulate milk proteins — which can be obtained from the stomach lining of young ruminants. The basic process of cheese making is simple enough that it may well have been discovered multiple times independently, whenever communities keeping domesticated cattle, sheep, or goats noticed that milk stored in animal-stomach containers naturally curdled and separated into solid curds and liquid whey. The ceramic vessels with perforated bases or slotted walls found at some Neolithic European sites have been interpreted as cheese strainers, providing indirect architectural evidence of the practice.

Cheese making is significant as part of the secondary products revolution — the gradual expansion of the range of uses to which domestic animals were put, from simple meat and hide production to milk, wool, traction, and transport. This expanded exploitation of domestic animals dramatically increased the economic return on the investment of maintaining herds and substantially increased the caloric and nutritional productivity of Neolithic economies.
